description: A full standalone pot of Nigerian honey beans — creamy, deeply seasoned with palm oil, crayfish, and smoked fish. The kind that makes people scrape the bottom of the pot. Pairs with anything or stands alone.

ingredients.name: honey beans (oloyin)
ingredients.note: Honey beans are the gold standard — naturally sweet and creamy. Brown beans work but are earthier. Black-eyed peas cook faster but the flavour is milder.

ingredients.name: palm oil
ingredients.note: This is non-negotiable. Do not substitute. The palm oil is the soul of Nigerian beans.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 roughly chopped for cooking the beans, 1 sliced for frying the base.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: Blend or roughly pound and add with the beans. 1 for mild, 2 for proper heat.

ingredients.name: ground crayfish
ingredients.note: The defining flavour of Nigerian beans. Use generously — do not skip or reduce.

ingredients.name: smoked fish
ingredients.note: Debone completely, flake into pieces, and add to the beans. Adds deep umami that cannot be replicated any other way.

ingredients.name: stockfish (okporoko)
ingredients.note: Soak in hot water for 30 minutes to soften. Adds a funky, deep flavour. Debone before adding.

ingredients.name: ponmo (cow skin)
ingredients.note: Must be already cooked soft before adding. Adds texture and richness.

ingredients.name: seasoning cubes
ingredients.note: Crush before adding. Add only when beans are fully soft.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Add ONLY when beans are fully soft and creamy. Salt before softness = beans that never fully soften.

ingredients.name: water
ingredients.note: Use hot water to top up during cooking. Cold water slows the cooking and can cause uneven texture.

ingredients.name: locust beans (iru/dawadawa)
ingredients.note: Fermented locust beans. Pungent and polarising but adds extraordinary depth to the beans. Rinse briefly before use.

steps.title: Sort, Wash and Soak
steps.instruction: Before anything touches heat, the beans need sorting, washing, and a proper soak — this is the groundwork that determines the final texture. Spread all 3 cups of beans on a flat surface or large plate and go through them carefully, picking out any small stones, shrivelled or discoloured beans, or debris. Rinse the sorted beans under cold running water three times until the water runs fairly clear. Transfer to a large bowl, cover generously with cold water — the beans will absorb a lot — and leave to soak for a minimum of 1 hour. Four hours is noticeably better, and an overnight soak produces the creamiest result with the shortest cook time. Drain and discard all soaking water before cooking.
micro_steps: Measure 3 cups of honey beans and spread them in a single layer on a flat plate or tray
Go through the beans slowly and remove any small stones, shrivelled beans, or debris — take your time, stones are a real hazard
Transfer the sorted beans to a sieve and rinse under cold running water
Rinse three times, swirling the beans with your hand each time, until the water runs fairly clear
Transfer the rinsed beans to a large bowl
Cover with cold water by at least 8–10cm — the beans will swell considerably
Leave to soak at room temperature for a minimum of 1 hour — 4 hours is better, overnight is best
After soaking, drain the beans completely through a sieve — the soaking water should be dark
Discard all soaking water — do not cook in it
Rinse the soaked beans once more under cold water before proceeding
prep_notes: Start the soak the night before if possible — overnight-soaked beans cook in noticeably less time and produce a creamier result
Put the stockfish to soak in hot water at the same time you start soaking the beans — it needs at least 30 minutes
Have a kettle ready for topping up with hot water during the long cook — cold water slows cooking significantly
Pre-cook the ponmo if using — it needs to be fully soft before it goes into the beans
steps.sensory_cue: Properly soaked beans should look noticeably plumper and larger than when dry — they should almost double in size after a good soak. The soaking water will be dark and slightly murky from the natural pigments releasing. The beans themselves should feel smooth and slightly softened on the outside when you run your thumb across one.
steps.failure_risk: Sorting for stones is not optional — a single small stone can chip a tooth. Take a full minute to go through the beans carefully on a flat surface where stones are easy to spot. Skipping or shortening the soak significantly increases cooking time and reduces the chances of achieving a truly creamy, smooth final texture.

steps.title: First Parboil and Drain
steps.instruction: This short parboil is a quick but important step that removes the compounds responsible for digestive discomfort and the faintly bitter, raw edge that unrinsed beans can carry into a finished dish. Place the soaked and drained beans in a pot, cover with plenty of fresh cold water — at least 5cm above the beans — and bring to a hard, rolling boil over high heat. Let them boil vigorously for 15 minutes without reducing the heat. The water will foam, bubble, and turn dark. Drain everything completely into a sieve, discard all of this water, and return the beans to the pot. They are now ready for the proper long cook.
micro_steps: Transfer the soaked, drained beans into a large pot
Pour in fresh cold water to cover the beans by at least 5cm
Place over high heat and bring to a hard rolling boil — this takes about 8–10 minutes
Once boiling vigorously, start a 15-minute timer — do not reduce the heat
The water will foam heavily — this is normal, do not skim it
After 15 minutes, remove from heat
Drain the beans completely through a sieve over the sink
The discarded water should be dark, foamy, and murky — this is what you are removing
Rinse the beans briefly under cold water
Return the drained beans to the same pot — ready for the main cook
steps.sensory_cue: The discarded parboil water should be dark brown, foamy, and slightly murky — if it looks relatively clear, the beans may not have boiled hard enough. After draining, the beans should look swollen and slightly softened on the outside, with the skins just beginning to loosen on some beans.
steps.failure_risk: Skipping this parboil step is the primary cause of bean-related digestive discomfort and also leaves a subtle bitterness in the finished dish. It adds only 15 minutes to the total time and the benefit is significant — do not skip it.

steps.title: Cook Beans Until Completely Soft
steps.instruction: This is the longest and most important stage — patience here is everything. Cover the parboiled beans with fresh hot water, at least 5cm above the beans, add the roughly chopped onion and scotch bonnet pepper, and bring to a boil over medium-high heat with the lid on. From this point, check the pot every 20 minutes: stir from the bottom, check the water level, and top up with hot water from the kettle whenever the level drops below the beans — never let them cook dry and never add cold water. Do not add salt, palm oil, or seasoning at any point during this stage. Cook until every single bean is completely, thoroughly soft — press one firmly between two fingers and it should crush to a completely smooth paste with zero resistance or grainy texture.
micro_steps: Pour fresh hot water into the pot over the drained beans — at least 5cm above the beans
Add 1 roughly chopped onion directly into the water
Add 2 scotch bonnet peppers — whole or roughly chopped
Cover the pot and bring to a boil over medium-high heat
Once boiling, reduce to a steady medium simmer — lid on
At the 20-minute mark, stir from the bottom and check the water level
If the water is at or below the level of the beans, add 1–2 cups of hot water from the kettle
Repeat the check and top-up every 20 minutes throughout the cook
Do not add salt, oil, or seasoning at any point during this stage
At the 60-minute mark, begin testing: press a single bean firmly between thumb and forefinger
If it crushes to a completely smooth paste with no resistance or gritty centre — the beans are ready
If any resistance remains, continue cooking and test every 15 minutes until fully soft
steps.sensory_cue: A perfectly cooked bean is uniformly creamy and smooth when pressed — no chalky centre, no graininess, no hard spot anywhere. The cooking liquid at this stage should look thick and starchy, almost like thin porridge. The pot should smell nutty, earthy, and distinctly bean-forward.
steps.failure_risk: This is the single most critical step in the entire recipe. Salt, palm oil, or any acidic ingredient added before the beans are completely soft will permanently lock the beans and prevent them from ever fully softening — no amount of additional cooking will fix it. Test multiple beans from different parts of the pot before you declare them done.

steps.title: Reduce to Desired Consistency
steps.instruction: With the beans fully soft, it is time to develop the texture you want before adding any flavour. Remove the lid and cook over medium heat, stirring regularly every 2 minutes. For a thick, creamy, almost mashed consistency — the classic Nigerian beans style — cook and stir until most of the excess liquid has evaporated and the beans begin breaking down into a thick, cohesive mass. Press some beans against the pot wall with the back of a spoon to speed this along. For a whole-bean style where each bean holds its shape but sits in a thick sauce — reduce just until the liquid is saucy and coats the beans, then stop. Either way, the beans and liquid should look unified, not separate.
micro_steps: Remove the lid from the pot once the beans are confirmed fully soft
Increase heat slightly to medium
Stir from the bottom every 2 minutes to prevent sticking
As you stir, press some beans against the side of the pot with the back of the spoon — this naturally thickens the liquid
For thick/mashed style: continue cooking and stirring for 10–15 minutes until the beans and liquid become one thick, creamy mass
For whole-bean style: cook for just 5–8 minutes until the liquid thickens to a sauce consistency and reduces to just below the level of the beans
Check consistency by dragging a spoon through the pot — it should leave a clear trail that closes slowly
If the beans become too thick before you have added flavour, add a small splash of hot water and stir
Remove from heat briefly while you prepare the palm oil base in the next step
steps.sensory_cue: The beans and cooking liquid should be coming together into a unified, thick, creamy mass rather than looking like beans floating in water. The smell is sweet, nutty, and intensely bean-forward at this point — a good smell. The colour should still be the natural brown-cream of the beans, not yet coloured by palm oil.
steps.failure_risk: Reducing too far without stirring causes the bottom to scorch quickly — beans have a high starch content and stick badly. Stir every 2 minutes without fail during the reduction. If the pot starts catching on the bottom, lower the heat immediately and add a splash of hot water.

steps.title: Fry the Palm Oil Base
steps.instruction: In a separate small pan — not the beans pot — heat the palm oil over medium heat until it melts to a clear, fluid orange. Do not let it get hot enough to smoke or darken. Add the sliced onion and fry gently, stirring occasionally, for 3–4 minutes until the onion is soft, translucent, and smelling sweet and rich in the palm oil. If using locust beans, add them now and fry for 1 minute — the smell will be intensely funky and pungent, which is exactly correct. Add the ground crayfish, stir for 30 seconds until it releases its sharp, savoury aroma into the oil, then remove the pan from heat. Pour this entire flavoured oil mixture directly into the beans pot and stir in thoroughly.
micro_steps: Place a small frying pan or saucepan over medium heat
Add 4 tablespoons of palm oil
Heat the oil until it melts and becomes a clear, fluid orange — do not let it smoke
Add the sliced onion rings and spread them out in the oil
Fry for 3–4 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the onion is soft and translucent
If using locust beans, add 1 tablespoon now and stir in — fry for 1 minute
Add 3 tablespoons of ground crayfish and stir immediately
Fry for 30 seconds — the crayfish will release a sharp, savoury, seafood aroma into the oil
Remove the pan from heat
Pour the entire contents of the pan — oil and all — directly into the beans pot
Stir vigorously to combine the palm oil base evenly through all the beans
steps.sensory_cue: Melted palm oil should be clear and orange — not dark, not smoking, not smell burnt. Onion frying in palm oil smells distinctly richer and more aromatic than onion in vegetable oil. When the crayfish hits the hot oil it releases a sharp, savoury, slightly oceanic aroma — this is the smell that signals the base is ready. The beans pot will immediately turn a deep orange-red when the base is poured in.
steps.failure_risk: Overheated palm oil turns dark brown, develops a harsh, acrid taste, and will make the entire pot of beans taste burnt and unpleasant with no way to fix it. Keep the heat at medium and watch closely — palm oil goes from perfect to ruined quickly. If the oil starts to smoke at any point, remove from heat immediately.

steps.title: Add Fish, Season and Finish
steps.instruction: With the palm oil base stirred through the beans, add the deboned and flaked smoked fish, the deboned stockfish, and the pre-cooked ponmo if using. Stir gently with a folding motion to distribute everything without breaking the fish into too-small pieces. Add the crushed seasoning cubes and stir in. Now add salt — but go slowly, because the smoked fish, stockfish, and crayfish have all contributed saltiness to the pot already. Add a small pinch, stir, taste, and repeat until the seasoning is right. Place the pot on low heat and cook for a final 15 minutes, stirring every 5 minutes, until all the flavours have fully merged and the beans look glossy and deeply orange from the palm oil. Final taste, adjust if needed, and serve.
micro_steps: Add the deboned, flaked smoked fish to the beans and fold in gently
Add the deboned stockfish pieces and fold in
Add pre-cooked, diced ponmo if using and fold in
Crush 2 seasoning cubes into powder and add — stir in thoroughly
Taste the beans before adding any salt — assess how much saltiness the fish has already contributed
Add salt in a very small pinch — no more than ¼ tsp at a time
Stir, wait 30 seconds, then taste again before deciding if more salt is needed
Repeat in small increments until the seasoning is balanced
Place the pot on low heat
Cook for 15 minutes, stirring gently every 5 minutes
Final taste — the beans should be deeply savoury, creamy, and have the palm oil, crayfish, and fish all present on the palate
Serve hot
steps.sensory_cue: Finished Nigerian beans should be deep orange-red from the palm oil, thick and creamy, with the smoked fish and other additions evenly distributed throughout. The smell should be layered and complex — palm oil, crayfish, and smoked fish all clearly present but in balance. One spoonful should taste rich, savoury, and deeply satisfying.
steps.failure_risk: Over-salting is the most common finishing mistake and it cannot be undone. Smoked fish, stockfish, and crayfish all carry significant salt — the beans may need far less added salt than you expect, sometimes none at all. Always taste before salting and add in the smallest increments possible.
